name: Bermuda Triangle
description: The Bermuda Triangle, also known as the Devil's Triangle, is a loosely defined region in the western part of the North Atlantic Ocean where a number of aircraft and ships are said to have disappeared under mysterious circumstances. Proponents suggest these disappearances are due to paranormal activity, extraterrestrial intervention, rogue waves, methane hydrates, or other unexplained phenomena. They often cite anecdotal evidence of sudden disappearances, compass malfunctions, and unusual weather patterns. Books and documentaries popularized the legend, portraying the area as a zone of inexplicable danger. However, mainstream scientific explanations attribute these disappearances to a combination of natural causes, such as severe weather (hurricanes, sudden storms), the Gulf Stream's strong currents, navigational errors, equipment malfunctions, and human error. Lloyd's of London, for example, has stated that the number of disappearances in the area is no greater than in any other similarly sized, heavily traveled area of the ocean. The US Navy and Coast Guard do not recognize the existence of the Bermuda Triangle as a geographically defined area of specific danger. Statistical analysis suggests the rate of incidents is proportional to the high volume of traffic in the region. Many disappearances attributed to the Triangle have been debunked or explained by conventional means, while others lack sufficient evidence to draw definitive conclusions.
subtitle: Area of unexplained disappearances and legends

claims.claim: Unexplained disappearances of ships and aircraft occur frequently in the area.
claims.evidence: Reports of missing vessels and planes; anecdotal accounts of strange occurrences.
claims.criticism: The number of disappearances is not statistically significant compared to other heavily trafficked areas. Many incidents are attributable to known causes like weather or human error.

claims.claim: Paranormal forces, such as extraterrestrial activity or vortexes, are responsible for the disappearances.
claims.evidence: Lack of wreckage in some cases; reports of unusual electromagnetic phenomena.
claims.criticism: Absence of credible scientific evidence for paranormal explanations; alternative explanations for electromagnetic anomalies exist.

claims.claim: Advanced technology from a lost civilization (Atlantis) interferes with navigation and causes disappearances.
claims.evidence: Speculation based on alleged underwater structures; theories about advanced ancient civilizations.
claims.criticism: Lack of concrete evidence for Atlantean technology; unsubstantiated claims about underwater structures being of artificial origin.

keyFigures.name: Vincent Gaddis
keyFigures.role: Popularized the term 'Bermuda Triangle' in a 1964 article.

keyFigures.name: Lawrence David Kusche
keyFigures.role: Author of 'The Bermuda Triangle Mystery – Solved,' which debunked many claims.

keyFigures.name: Charles Berlitz
keyFigures.role: Popularized the Bermuda Triangle myth with sensationalized books.

mythologyConnections: Poseidon/Neptune (god of the sea, controlling the elements)

timeline.event: E.V.W. Jones first mentions unexplained disappearances in the area in an Associated Press article.
Vincent Gaddis coins the term 'Bermuda Triangle' in an Argosy magazine article.
Charles Berlitz publishes 'The Bermuda Triangle,' popularizing the legend.
Lawrence David Kusche publishes 'The Bermuda Triangle Mystery – Solved,' debunking many claims.

sources.author: United States Coast Guard
sources.work: Official Statement on the Bermuda Triangle
sources.title: United States Coast Guard
